beats.narration_text: Cześć — Liam, in for Bear. collage-ads: the end-to-end pipeline for halftone paper-collage and stop-motion-graphic ads. Decode a reference into an editable spec, generate on-brand stills with nano-banana-2, animate with Seedance 2.0 — all on the Arcads connector. Two halves: Phase A analyzes, Phase B builds.

props.runningText: Phase A: decode → spec | Phase B: stills → motion...
output: Phase A: decode reference → collage JSON spec
Phase B: nano-banana-2 stills → Seedance 2.0 assembly motion
Every spec field independently editable for variations
One model per delivered set — consistency = brand

beats.narration_text: Anatomy. One pipeline, two halves. Phase A: analyze — decode a reference into the collage JSON spec. Medium, color field, elements, idea, label — all independently specified fields. Phase B: build — stills on nano-banana-2 from the spec, then assembly-from-empty motion on Seedance 2.0. The spec is the centerpiece: every field is editable so any variation request — same look, change the color field — rewrites one block.

props.title: Decode → spec → stills → assembly motion.
files: SKILL.md — Phase A decode format, Phase B build flow, hard defaults
collage JSON spec — the centerpiece: every field independently editable

beats.narration_text: Pipeline. Phase A: decode the reference in passes — medium first, then color field, then each cut-out element, then the idea, then the label. Output: breakdown, generic prompt, and collage JSON spec. Phase B: generate finished stills on nano-banana-2 per scene — burn the label into the prompt. Phase B3: Seedance takes the finished still as a reference and a prompt describing the build; the clip opens on the empty field and the cut-out pieces slide in one by one. Phase B4: lock the look, vary only the idea across scenes.

props.title: Analyze → spec → stills → Seedance motion → scale.

phases.label: PHASE A
phases.note: decode in passes: medium → color field → elements → idea → label → JSON spec

phases.label: B1 BRIEF
phases.note: lock: assets, duration (5/10/15s), format (9:16/1:1/16:9), scene count, idea-per-scene

phases.label: B2 STILLS
phases.note: arcads_generate_image_nano_banana (model: nano-banana-2) — burn label in prompt, not overlay

phases.label: B3 MOTION
phases.note: arcads_generate_video_seedance_20 — finished still + build prompt → assembly-from-empty clip

phases.label: B4 SCALE
phases.note: lock look once; vary only idea per scene; one model per delivered set

props.footerNote: Kling 3.0 Pro (startFrame+endFrame) as escape hatch when Seedance build must be exact.

beats.narration_text: Self-demo. Phase A is purely free — no Arcads calls. Watch Claude decode a hypothetical halftone coffee-brand ad into the collage JSON spec. Three passes: medium and craft, color field, elements.

props.runningText: medium → color field → elements → idea → label → JSON...
output: Reference: cobalt-blue field, halftone paper coffee cup
Halftone sun cut-out, burned label 'MORNING BREW'
Phase A only — no Arcads, no nano-banana-2 spend
Output: collage JSON spec (every field independently editable)

beats.narration_text: Cobalt field, halftone paper coffee cup, halftone sun, burned label MORNING BREW. JSON spec: medium layered-collage; craft style halftone paper-cut stop-motion. Color field: #1B4FD8 cobalt, even, slight paper grain. Element one: white coffee cup, halftone at 40 lpi, crisp cut edge, 4-pixel soft shadow, center-left. Element two: yellow-orange sun, halftone paper-texture cut, upper-right. Composition: cup low-center, sun upper-right as counterpoint, generous negative space between. Idea: morning warmth on bold cobalt. Label: MORNING BREW, bold condensed sans, top-center, burned in at generation — never via arcads add text overlay.

props.title: Phase A — collage JSON spec: MORNING BREW coffee ad

beats.narration_text: Mechanism one. The driving question is: if I had to regenerate this exact frame from scratch on nano-banana-2, what would I need to specify? That question drives Phase A. Every observation needs a technique and a why — 'has texture' is nothing. 'Visible halftone dot pattern, dense at 45 lpi, reads as printed-magazine craft' is reproducible. Over-analyze on purpose — the next request is almost always a variation, and that only works if every attribute is independently swappable.

props.heading: Every attribute independently swappable.
props.body: Phase A mindset: 'If I had to regenerate this exact frame from scratch on nano-banana-2, what would I need to specify?' Over-analyze: 'has texture' → nothing. 'visible halftone dot pattern, dense at 45 lpi, reads as printed-magazine craft instead of flat digital' → reproducible. This is why each JSON spec field is independently editable — a 'change the color field only' request rewrites one block and leaves everything else.
props.quote: The driving question is: 'If I had to regenerate this exact frame from scratch on nano-banana-2, what would I need to specify?'

beats.narration_text: Mechanism two. Labels are burned in at generation, never via arcads add text overlay. The label is part of the image prompt — nano-banana-2 burns it in as printed craft text matching the paper-collage style. The overlay tool breaks the craft texture and is never called in this pipeline. If a label degrades on generation, reroll with a different seed or higher n-B-generations. Hand off persistent label problems to Figma — but do not reach for the overlay tool.

props.heading: Burn the label in. Never overlay it.
props.body: Hard rule from SKILL.md: 'Never call arcads_add_text_overlay.' The label is burned into nano-banana-2's prompt ('Burned-in label MORNING BREW, bold condensed sans, top-center' is part of the image prompt). It renders as printed craft text — consistent with the paper-collage texture. The overlay tool adds a flat digital text layer that breaks the medium. If the label keeps degrading: reroll (nbGenerations) or hand off to Figma. Not the overlay tool.
props.quote: Labels are burned in at generation, never via arcads_add_text_overlay.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism three. Consistency across scenes is what makes it read as a brand rather than one-offs. Phase B4 is the scaling law: lock the look once — color field, halftone and cut convention, label treatment — then vary only the idea per scene. Same model across the set. Switching from Seedance to Kling mid-set silently breaks the paper-texture consistency even if individual clips look fine.

props.heading: Lock the look. Vary the idea. One model.
props.body: Phase B4 scaling law: lock color_field + halftone+cut convention + label_treatment across all scenes. Vary: elements and idea per scene. One model per delivered set: 'Mixing nano/gpt for stills or Seedance/Kling for clips breaks the paper-texture and color consistency that makes the set look intentional.' Kling 3.0 Pro (startFrame + endFrame) is available per-clip when Seedance's build is not exact enough — but use it only for those clips, not silently across the set.
props.quote: Consistency across scenes is what makes it read as a brand rather than one-offs.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Claude, Collaged. collage-ads delivers the decode-to-animate pipeline: Phase A makes every attribute independently swappable; Phase B generates stills and assembles them into motion. One model per set. Consistency makes it brand.

artifactLines: Phase A: decode reference → collage JSON spec (every field swappable)
Phase B: nano-banana-2 stills → Seedance assembly-from-empty motion
Labels burned in at image generation — never via text overlay
One model per set — Seedance or Kling, not both

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run collage-ads on any reference image. Two questions before you start: is your Phase A JSON spec specific enough that you could reroll any single field independently? And have you decided which model — Seedance or Kling — you will use for every clip in the set?

props.runningText: Phase A spec specific? model decided for whole set?
output: Is your JSON spec specific enough to reroll any one field?
Which model — Seedance or Kling — for every clip?
